mcqs.question: How is a 2D array stored in memory?
mcqs.explanation: In C, two-dimensional arrays are stored in row-major order, meaning all elements of the first row are stored first, followed by elements of the second row, and so on.
arr[1][2] accesses the element at row 1, column 2. Following zero-based indexing, this is the third element (6) in the second row {4, 5, 6}.

mcqs.question: Which declaration is correct for initializing a 2D array with 2 rows and 3 columns?
mcqs.explanation: When initializing a 2D array, you can omit the row dimension but must specify the column dimension. Option C correctly declares a 2D array with 2 rows and 3 columns.

mcqs.question: What is the equivalent pointer notation for accessing arr[i][j] in a 2D array?
mcqs.explanation: The correct pointer notation for arr[i][j] is *(*(arr + i) + j). Here, (arr + i) points to the i-th row, and *(arr + i) + j points to the j-th element in that row.
The code sets elements on the main diagonal (where row index equals column index) to 1. arr[1][1] is on the main diagonal, so it will be set to 1.
